questions.stem: A patient reports chest pressure and becomes hypotensive and tachycardic. What is the most useful bedside interpretation?

options.text: The patient may be having an acute cardiovascular emergency
The pain is likely harmless because the patient is still awake
The heart rate change is the only issue
The blood pressure will likely fix itself without reassessment

questions.rationale: Chest pressure with hypotension and tachycardia is concerning for an unstable cardiac event and requires immediate reassessment. **Why the others are wrong:** - B Awake status does not make the chest pain safe. - C The whole pattern matters, not just the heart rate. - D Waiting for spontaneous correction is unsafe.

distractorRationales.b: This distractor shifts care toward premature attribution instead of circulation and perfusion support. The stem cue "A patient reports chest pressure and becomes hypotensive and tachycardic" makes circulation or perfusion the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.c: "The heart rate change is the only issue" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "Chest pressure with hypotension and tachycardia is concerning for an unstable cardiac event and requires immediate reassessment" raises concern for circulation or perfusion, so circulation and perfusion support is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor shifts care toward delayed assessment instead of circulation and perfusion support. The stem cue "Chest pain plus unstable vitals is a high-risk pattern" makes circulation or perfusion the active safety concern.

questions.takeaway: Chest pain plus unstable vitals is a high-risk pattern.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: A toddler has had vomiting and diarrhea for two days. Which finding should the nurse recognize as most concerning for dehydration?

options.text: Fewer wet diapers than usual
Asking for water
Mild irritability at nap time
A normal appetite earlier in the week

questions.rationale: Decreased urine output is a key dehydration clue in young children and should be taken seriously. **Why the others are wrong:** - B Asking for water is not the strongest warning sign. - C Mild irritability is nonspecific. - D A prior normal appetite does not answer the current question.

distractorRationales.b: "Asking for water" moves to activity, intake, or discharge planning too early. The cue "Decreased urine output is a key dehydration clue in young children and should be taken seriously" raises concern for renal injury, so renal protection is needed before ambulation, intake, or discharge teaching.
distractorRationales.c: The safer response addresses renal injury; "Mild irritability at nap time" moves care toward reassurance before renal protection.
distractorRationales.d: The safer response addresses renal injury; "A normal appetite earlier in the week" moves care toward reassurance before renal protection.

questions.takeaway: In children, fewer wet diapers can be an important dehydration sign.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: A patient with shortness of breath is now using accessory muscles and can only speak two or three words at a time. What should the nurse do first?

options.text: Reassess breathing, oxygenation, and work of breathing immediately
Ask the patient to rest before checking vital signs
Wait to see whether the shortness of breath improves on its own
Focus on the lung sounds later in the shift

questions.rationale: Accessory muscle use and short phrases suggest rising respiratory distress. The nurse should reassess the patient right away. **Why the others are wrong:** - B Rest does not replace urgent assessment. - C Delay can miss a worsening airway or breathing problem. - D Lung sounds are important, but not later in the shift.

distractorRationales.b: "Ask the patient to rest before checking vital signs" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "Accessory muscle use and short phrases suggest rising respiratory distress" raises concern for oxygenation or ventilation, so airway and breathing intervention is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.c: The option can leave oxygenation or ventilation untreated because it treats active deterioration as something safe to watch. "Reassess breathing, oxygenation, and work of breathing immediately" fits the safer response to speech limitation plus accessory muscle use is a respiratory warning sign.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor shifts care toward delayed assessment instead of airway and breathing intervention. The stem cue "- C Delay can miss a worsening airway or breathing problem" makes oxygenation or ventilation the active safety concern.

questions.takeaway: Speech limitation plus accessory muscle use is a respiratory warning sign.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: Which finding should the nurse treat as the most urgent?

options.text: New unilateral arm weakness
A patient asking for a blanket
Mild boredom during a long wait
A patient wanting the television volume adjusted

questions.rationale: New one-sided weakness can signal stroke and needs immediate attention. **Why the others are wrong:** - B This is nonurgent. - C Boredom is not a clinical change. - D Comfort adjustments are not the highest priority.

distractorRationales.b: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of neurologic safety assessment. The stem cue "- D Comfort adjustments are not the highest priority" makes neurologic deterioration the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.c: The option can leave neurologic deterioration untreated because it treats active deterioration as something safe to watch. "New unilateral arm weakness" fits the safer response to new one-sided weakness can signal stroke and needs immediate attention.
distractorRationales.d: "A patient wanting the television volume adjusted" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "Sudden one-sided weakness should always be treated as urgent" raises concern for neurologic deterioration, so neurologic safety assessment is needed before routine care.

questions.takeaway: Sudden one-sided weakness should always be treated as urgent.

questions.stem: A patient becomes cool, pale, and diaphoretic with a weakening pulse and dropping urine output. What is the best interpretation?

options.text: Circulation may be unstable and perfusion may be worsening
The patient is stable because the skin is not blue
Urine output is unrelated to circulation
These findings are expected if the patient is anxious

questions.rationale: Cool skin, weak pulse, and falling urine output can reflect poor perfusion and a worsening cardiovascular problem. **Why the others are wrong:** - B Cyanosis is not required for instability. - C Urine output is a useful perfusion marker. - D Anxiety does not explain the full perfusion pattern.

distractorRationales.b: "The patient is stable because the skin is not blue" commits to a competing cause before checking the dangerous pattern. The cue "Cold skin and dropping urine output can signal poor circulation" raises concern for renal injury, so renal protection is needed before diagnostic reassurance.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of renal protection. The stem cue "diaphoretic with a weakening pulse and dropping urine output" makes renal injury the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.d: The safer response addresses renal injury; "These findings are expected if the patient is anxious" moves care toward reassurance before renal protection.

questions.takeaway: Cold skin and dropping urine output can signal poor circulation.

visualRationale.title: unstable_perfusion
visualRationale.caption: Cold skin and dropping urine output can signal poor circulation.
visualRationale.conclusion: Cold skin and dropping urine output can signal poor circulation.

questions.stem: A client reports severe eye pain, blurred vision, and seeing halos around lights. Which interpretation by the nurse is most accurate?

options.text: The client may have acute angle-closure glaucoma and needs urgent evaluation.
This is an expected effect of routine eye fatigue.
The client is likely having a migraine and can rest at home.
These findings show that prescribed eye drops are working well.

questions.rationale: Severe eye pain with blurred vision and halos is concerning for acute angle-closure glaucoma, an ocular emergency that can threaten vision. The nurse should recognize the need for urgent ophthalmic evaluation rather than dismissing the symptoms as routine strain. Time-sensitive treatment matters for outcome.

distractorRationales.b: "This is an expected effect of routine eye fatigue" treats active deterioration as something safe to watch. The cue "A client reports severe eye pain, blurred vision" raises concern for clinical safety risk, so bedside safety action is needed before routine observation.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor shifts care toward premature activity instead of bedside safety action. The stem cue "A client reports severe eye pain, blurred vision" makes clinical safety risk the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.d: The option can leave clinical safety risk untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "The client may have acute angle-closure glaucoma and needs urgent evaluation" fits the safer response to severe eye pain with blurred vision and halos is concerning for acute angle-closure glaucoma, an ocular emergency.

questions.takeaway: Eye pain plus halos and blurred vision should trigger concern for acute angle-closure glaucoma.

questions.stem: A client with adrenal insufficiency has vomiting, weakness, blood pressure 82/48 mmHg, and sodium 128 mEq/L. Which provider prescription should the nurse implement first?

options.text: Administer IV hydrocortisone and isotonic fluids.
Restrict oral fluids and monitor weight.
Hold corticosteroids until the morning cortisol level is drawn.
Teach the client about low-sodium foods.

questions.rationale: Hypotension, hyponatremia, vomiting, and weakness in a client with adrenal insufficiency suggest adrenal crisis. The priority is rapid steroid replacement and volume support to prevent circulatory collapse. Delaying treatment for further testing can be life-threatening.

distractorRationales.b: "Restrict oral fluids and monitor weight" misses the bedside risk because sodium 128 mEq/L points to renal injury. Renal protection depends on hydration, medication review, and creatinine follow-up before assuming the study is low risk.
distractorRationales.c: "Hold corticosteroids until the morning cortisol level is drawn" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "The priority is rapid steroid replacement and volume support to prevent circulatory collapse" raises concern for renal injury, so renal protection is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.d: The option can leave renal injury untreated because it uses teaching before the physiologic risk is controlled. "Administer IV hydrocortisone and isotonic fluids" fits the safer response to hypotension, hyponatremia, vomiting.

questions.takeaway: Suspected adrenal crisis requires steroids and isotonic fluids right away.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: A client receiving IV antibiotics suddenly develops wheezing, widespread hives, and swelling of the lips. Which action should the nurse take first?

options.text: Stop the infusion and prepare to administer epinephrine.
Obtain a full allergy history before intervening.
Slow the infusion to reduce the reaction.
Document the event and continue monitoring.

questions.rationale: Wheezing, hives, and lip swelling indicate anaphylaxis. The causative infusion should be stopped immediately and epinephrine readied or administered per protocol because airway compromise and shock can develop quickly. Delaying definitive treatment can be fatal.

distractorRationales.b: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of airway and breathing intervention. The stem cue "A client receiving IV antibiotics suddenly develops wheezing, widespread hives" makes oxygenation or ventilation the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.c: The safer response addresses oxygenation or ventilation; "Slow the infusion to reduce the reaction" moves care toward routine care before airway and breathing intervention.
distractorRationales.d: "Document the event and continue monitoring" treats active deterioration as something safe to watch. The cue "The causative infusion should be stopped immediately and epinephrine readied or administered per protocol because airway compromise" raises concern for oxygenation or ventilation, so airway and breathing intervention is needed before routine observation.

questions.takeaway: When anaphylaxis begins, stop the trigger and act for airway and circulation immediately.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: A client with a T4 spinal cord injury suddenly reports a pounding headache and has a blood pressure of 208/110 mmHg. Which nursing action is the priority?

options.text: Sit the client upright and assess for bladder distention.
Lay the client flat and increase the room temperature.
Ask the client to rest quietly until the headache passes.
Offer oral fluids and reassess in 30 minutes.

questions.rationale: Severe hypertension and headache in a client with a high spinal cord injury suggest autonomic dysreflexia, often triggered by bladder distention. The nurse should sit the client upright to lower blood pressure and remove the triggering stimulus right away. Delay increases the risk of stroke or seizure.

distractorRationales.b: The option can leave neurologic deterioration untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "Sit the client upright and assess for bladder distention" fits the safer response to a client with a T4 spinal cord injury suddenly reports a pounding headache and has a blood pressure.
distractorRationales.c: "Ask the client to rest quietly until the headache passes" misses the bedside risk because delay increases the risk of stroke or seizure points to neurologic deterioration. Neurologic change needs prompt assessment and safety measures before reassurance, discharge, or routine reassessment.
distractorRationales.d: The safer response addresses neurologic deterioration; "Offer oral fluids and reassess in 30 minutes" moves care toward routine observation before neurologic safety assessment.

questions.takeaway: Autonomic dysreflexia is a remove-the-trigger emergency, often starting with sitting the client up and checking the bladder.

questions.stem: A nurse begins a packed red blood cell transfusion for a client with postoperative anemia. Fifteen minutes later the client reports chills, low back pain, and chest tightness. Which action is the priority?

options.text: Stop the transfusion and keep the IV line open with normal saline.
Slow the transfusion rate and reassess in 5 minutes.
Administer acetaminophen and continue the transfusion.
Document the symptoms as an expected febrile response.

questions.rationale: Chills, low back pain, and chest tightness shortly after starting blood are red-flag findings for an acute transfusion reaction. The nurse should stop the blood immediately to prevent further exposure and maintain IV access with normal saline using new tubing so emergency treatment can be given. Slowing the infusion or masking symptoms delays the response to a potentially life-threatening complication.

distractorRationales.b: "Slow the transfusion rate and reassess in 5 minutes" treats active deterioration as something safe to watch. The cue "chest tightness shortly after starting blood are red-flag findings for an acute transfusion reaction" raises concern for circulation or perfusion, so circulation and perfusion support is needed before routine observation.
distractorRationales.c: The option can leave circulation or perfusion untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "Stop the transfusion and keep the IV line open with normal saline" fits the safer response to a nurse begins a packed red blood cell transfusion for a client with postoperative anemia.
distractorRationales.d: "Document the symptoms as an expected febrile response" normalizes a finding that needs reassessment. The cue "Suspected transfusion reaction means stop the blood first and keep the line open with normal saline" raises concern for circulation or perfusion, so circulation and perfusion support is needed before reassurance.

questions.takeaway: Suspected transfusion reaction means stop the blood first and keep the line open with normal saline.

questions.stem: A client with profuse diarrhea tests positive for Clostridioides difficile. Which infection-control action should the nurse implement?

options.text: Use soap and water for hand hygiene after removing gloves.
Use alcohol-based sanitizer alone because it is faster.
Place the client on droplet precautions only.
Share the blood pressure cuff after wiping it with a dry towel.

questions.rationale: C. difficile spores are not reliably removed by alcohol-based hand rub alone, so soap-and-water hand hygiene is required after glove removal. Contact precautions and dedicated equipment are also important. Using only sanitizer or shared equipment increases transmission risk.

distractorRationales.b: The option can leave infection progression untreated because it commits to a competing cause before checking the dangerous pattern. "Use soap and water for hand hygiene after removing gloves" fits the safer response to c diff spore precautions infection.
distractorRationales.c: Because c diff spore precautions infection suggests infection progression, "Place the client on droplet precautions only" would not correct the active problem. Infection control or sepsis escalation limits spread and organ hypoperfusion before lower-acuity care.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor shifts care toward premature attribution instead of infection or sepsis escalation. The stem cue "c diff spore precautions infection" makes infection progression the active safety concern.

questions.takeaway: C. difficile care requires soap-and-water hand hygiene and contact precautions.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: Which action by the nurse best helps prevent a central line-associated bloodstream infection in a hospitalized client?

options.text: Scrub the hub before each access and maintain a sterile dressing.
Change the dressing only when the client reports discomfort.
Use the line for routine blood draws to avoid extra venipunctures.
Leave one port uncapped between intermittent infusions.

questions.rationale: Consistent hub disinfection and sterile dressing care are core nursing actions for preventing central line infections. Using the line casually or leaving it uncapped increases contamination risk. Dressing changes should follow evidence-based timing and contamination rules, not patient comfort alone.

distractorRationales.b: Because dressing changes should follow evidence-based timing and contamination rules, not patient comfort alone suggests infection progression, "Change the dressing only when the client reports discomfort" would not correct the active problem. Infection control or sepsis escalation limits spread and organ hypoperfusion before lower-acuity care.
distractorRationales.c: The option can leave infection progression untreated because it treats active deterioration as something safe to watch. "Scrub the hub before each access and maintain a sterile dressing" fits the safer response to preventing CLABSI depends on aseptic access and sterile site care every time.
distractorRationales.d: Because dressing changes should follow evidence-based timing and contamination rules, not patient comfort alone suggests infection progression, "Leave one port uncapped between intermittent infusions" would not correct the active problem. Infection control or sepsis escalation limits spread and organ hypoperfusion before lower-acuity care.

questions.takeaway: Preventing CLABSI depends on aseptic access and sterile site care every time.

visualRationale.caption: Preventing CLABSI depends on aseptic access and sterile site care every time.
